subsection.title: Definition
subsection.p: Let A be an n\times n matrix.
The scalar \lambda is the eigenvalue.

subsection.title: Meaning
subsection.p: An eigenvector is a direction that A does not turn.
The matrix A only stretches, shrinks, or reverses that direction.

subsection.title: Finding eigenvalues
subsection.p:
Start with
A\mathbf v=\lambda\mathbf v.
Rewrite:
(A-\lambda I)\mathbf v=\mathbf 0.
For a nonzero solution \mathbf v, the matrix A-\lambda I must be singular:
\boxed{ \det(A-\lambda I)=0. }
This equation is the characteristic equation.

subsection.title: Characteristic polynomial
2\times2 shortcut
Finding eigenvectors
subsection.p: The solution space is the eigenspace for \lambda.

subsection.title: Diagonal matrices
subsection.p:
If
D= \begin{pmatrix} \lambda_1\amp0\amp\cdots\amp0\\ 0\amp\lambda_2\amp\cdots\amp0\\ \vdots\amp\vdots\amp\ddots\amp\vdots\\ 0\amp0\amp\cdots\amp\lambda_n \end{pmatrix},
then the standard basis vectors are eigenvectors:
\boxed{ D\mathbf e_i=\lambda_i\mathbf e_i. }

subsection.title: Diagonalization
subsection.p:
If A has n linearly independent eigenvectors
\mathbf v_1,\ldots,\mathbf v_n,
then put them into a matrix
\boxed{ P= \begin{pmatrix} |\amp|\amp\amp|\\ \mathbf v_1\amp\mathbf v_2\amp\cdots\amp\mathbf v_n\\ |\amp|\amp\amp| \end{pmatrix}. }

subsection.title: Powers
Symmetric matrices
subsection.p: Symmetric matrices are the clean case. Hessian matrices are symmetric when mixed partials agree.

warning.p: A matrix may fail to have enough eigenvectors to diagonalize.
Eigenvalues are defined only for square matrices.
For real matrices, complex eigenvalues may occur unless the matrix is symmetric.
